Memorizing transformers. In International Conference on Learning Representations, 2022. [43] Manzil Zaheer, Guru Guruganesh, Kumar Avinava Dubey, Joshua Ainslie, Chris Alberti, Santiago Ontanon, Philip Pham, Anirudh Ravula, Qifan Wang, Li Yang, et al. Big bird: Transformers for longer sequences. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 33, 2020. 13A Limitations While BST ’s SSM layer allows the model to unroll and parallelize the recurrence that models long- term context between blocks of tokens, the SSM variants are reliant on efficient FFT operations. We have found that the FFT operation is an important speed bottleneck on TPUs that needs to be resolved to better scale BST to many layers and larger models. While we are still investigating the reasons, we found that JAX FFT was 4× faster on GPUs. Further, new SSM variants such as S5 [34] bypass FFT operations using a binary associative operator4. Our implementation is modular enough that we can simply plug in S5 or use other FFT implementations. One of our assumptions is that BST’s SSM layer is able to capture the right long-term dependencies for each block. The SSM recurrence at step T = t provides a summarized representation of previous steps for T = 0 to T = t. However, a single vector representation may not be powerful enough to support all important long-term dependencies. Despite the perplexity improvements on long-range language modeling tasks, this assumption needs to be tested on other long-range classification tasks such as Long Range Arena [37] as well. It is possible that our model can perform better if we feed to the attention layer k = W SSM representations that are chosen by a top-k retrieval operation, similar to the one in Memorizing Transformer [42]. B More detailed comparisons with existing baselines This section provides the reader with a more in-depth comparison with similar architectures. We cover BRECT [21] in Section B.1 and GSS-H YBRID [28] in Section B.2. B.1 Comparison with Block Recurrent Transformer ( BRECT) The Block Transformer sublayer (i.e SLIDE :12L ) processes keys and values from the previous window stored in a differentiable cache. This is implemented similarly to the sliding window attention pattern suggested in [21] and was originally introduced by Transformer-XL [ 8]. Using a causal mask, at every token inference step, the attention mechanism is applied to blocks of tokens of size W and is partially extended to the cached keys and values from the previous block with the sliding window. BRECT, as explained in [21], uses a non-differentiable cache that is carried from one sequence of size L to the next5. The last recurrent states of a sequence are stored in a non-differentiable cache and fed to the next training step on the following sequence in the document as a warm-start. We do not pass such a representation, since to compute the output of the convolution, we need access to the whole sequence. We believe that this is one advantage that BRECT has over our method, especially for very long examples that split into ordered sequences of length L, since the cache carried from one sequence to the next can provide very useful long-range information and (weak) access to the whole past. Since we need the whole sequence to compute SSM states, history beyond L may be lost in the process. We believe that BST can further be improved by adding non-differentiable sequence cache for very long documents. While in other architectures, the history between blocks of tokens is not modeled, both BST and BRECT use a mechanism to model previous block context. The authors of BRECT experiment with various sequential gating mechanisms to condense the information from past blocks. With BST , we use SSM to provide context from previous blocks to the current block as explained in Section 3.2. B.2 Comparison with the Transformer GSS-H YBRID GSS-H YBRID [28] is a SSM-Transformer hybrid architecture that we first describe in Section 4.1. The architecture is significantly different from BST . GSS-H YBRID is primarily composed of Gated State Space (GSS ) layers and has a few interleaved Transformer layers at every 4th layer starting with the 2nd layer. BST on the other hand is mainly composed of Block Transformer layers and has Block-State Transformer layers at positions {1, 7, 9} for the ∼200M model and {1, 5, 7, 9} for the ∼400M model. Our hybrid does not stack SSM and Transformer layers like the GSS-H YBRID but rather replaces the recurrence in BRECT with an SSM such as S4. In BST, the SSM generates states 4In JAX, this is equivalent to using jax.lax.associative_scan. 5In our work and in [21], a document is split into multiple sequences of size L and each sequence is split into multiple blocks of size W 14for each Block Transformer representations and we then use cross-attention to mix the states and the self-attention outputs. The authors in [ 28] initially built GSS , a gated version of DSS [16], to (1) reduce SSM parameter dimensions, (2) stabilize training of the SSM and (3) allow better length generalization. However, when experimenting with SSMs such as S4 or DSS , we found that the gating was not necessary to achieve all three objectives stated above. We decided that usingGSS ’s Gated Attention Unit [20] was therefore not needed when integrating SSM states into the attention mechanism. We also reiterate that the authors in [ 28] used hyperparameter search to get the best performance while we did not. C Scaling Experiments Number of parameters Perplexity 8 10 12 14 16 80M 200M 320M 650M 1.3B Trsf-XL:2048 Rec:fixed:skip BST:SH:unstruct Figure 5: Scaling properties on PG-19. Yellow: (BST:SH: UNSTRUCT ) 12-layer Block-State Transformer. Red: (R EC:FIXED :SKIP ) 12-layer Block-Recurrent Transformer. Blue: (T RSF -XL-2048) 13-layer Transformer-XL. In this section, we compare how BST scales compared to Transformer-XL with 4× the window size and BRECT. In Figure 5, we see that at lower scales, from 80M to 200M, BRECT and BST have very similar performances. Beyond 200M, the perplexity performance percentage gap between BRECT and BST increases from 2.5% at 200M paramaters to 4.0% at 1.3B parameters. The perplexity performance percentage gap between BRECT and TRSF -XL is even more pronounced as it starts at 7.6% at 200M parameters to 10.6% at 1.3B parameters. D Long Range Arena Experiments MODEL LIST OPTS TEXT RETRIEVAL IMAGE PATHFINDER PATH-X AVG Transformer 36.37 64.27 57.46 42.44 71.40 ✗ 53.66 Linear Trans. 16.13 65.90 53.09 42.34 75.30 ✗ 50.46 Reformer 37.27 56.10 53.40 38.07 68.50 ✗ 50.56 Performer 18.01 65.40 53.82 42.77 77.05 ✗ 51.18 BigBird 36.05 64.02 59.29 40.83 74.87 ✗ 54.17 Mega 63.14 90.43 91.25 90.44 96.01 97.98 88.21 S4D 60.47 86.18 89.46 88.19 93.06 91.95 84.89 S4 59.60 86.82 90.90 88.65 94.20 96.35 86.09 S5 62.15 89.32 91.40 88.00 95.33 98.58 87.46 Methods with chunked input sequences BRECT:FIXED :SKIP 37.29 66.14 58.76 50.41 76.33 75.89 60.80 MEGA -CHUNK 58.76 90.19 90.97 85.80 94.41 93.81 85.66 BST:SH:S4 (ours) 61.49 87.63 90.51 91.07 95.75 95.28 86.96 Table 2: Performance on Long-Range Arena (LRA). For a fair comparison, we adjust the number of layers and model dimensions on each task so that BST and BRECT have similar number of parameters with S4 and MEGA -CHUNK . BR ECT results are from our own runs and all other baselines are from published results. 15While the main focus of our research was to demonstrate that hybrid Transformer-SSM models are efficient and perform well on long context autoregressive LM, we also evaluate our method on standard classification task where long range dependencies in a sequence are important to capture. In Table 2, we present our results on the Long Range Arena (LRA) benchmark [38] which incorporates three different modalities including text, images, and mathematical expressions. The LRA dataset also tests models on various sequence lengths from 1K to 16K. BST:SH:S4 is composed of four BST layers (no BRT layers are interleaved) and two S4 layers on top. We use the same standard block length of 512 for BST and BRT. However, we train BST and BRT on the full sequences (up to 16K for Path-X). We use AdamW as our optimizer [ 24] with a warmup for the learning rate, where we start from a value of 1e−7 and increase the learning rate linearly up a specified value ∈ {1e−3, 2e−3, 4e−3} for the first 10% of training. This is followed by cosine annealing for the rest of training down to a value of 1e−7. All layers are bidirectional, including the S4 layer in BST:SH:S4 as described in [13]. Our weight decay is chosen from {0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15} and our dropout is chosen from {0, 0.1}. Except for Path-X experiments, we use weight decays ∈ {0.03, 0.05, 0.07} for all parameters except S4D matrices A and B. Also, for Path-X, the initialization range of our discretization time step ∆ for PathX is decreased from (∆min, ∆max) = (0.001, 0.1) to (∆min, ∆max) = (0.0001, 0.01). Our results on LRA are very promissing and show that, compared to other state-of the art methods that chunk sequences into blocks, BST is able to model long range dependencies. For example, BST outperforms MEGA -CHUNK [27] on four out of six LRA tasks and by 1.5% on the average score. However, BST still needs to improve (perhaps by extending the block size) to catch up to MEGA (without chunks). E Ablation Studies In the following section, we perform ablations to investigate (1) the placement of a single SSM layer in Table 3 in the overall architecture, (2) the effects of the number of SSM layers added in Table 4, and (3) the size D of the SSM state in Table 5. For the ablations, we use the ∼200M parameter BST:SH:S4 , since it is the fastest model, and assess various configurations on PG19. Table 3: A single BST at various layer index. Layer index Perplexity 3 12.41 7 11.92 9 11.88 12 12.03 Table 4: Multiple BST layers at various locations. Num layers Perplexity 2 11.69 3 11.57 4 11.21 5 11.20 Table 5: Increasing BST’s S4 model state size D. State Size Perplexity Step Time 8 11.95 ×0.7 16 11.57 ×1.0 32 11.55 ×1.8 64 11.54 ×3.2 In Table 3, we experiment adding a single BST layer at layer indices 3, 6, 9, 12. We notice that a single BST layer with state size D = 16 located closer to the middle of the whole Block Transformer stack, at index = 9, has the greatest effect on perplexity. This finding is inline with findings in prior work [42, 21]. In Table 4, we test if adding multiple BST layers yields improvements on performance. We start with BST layers with state size D = 16 at indices 0, 9. We follow by adding another BST layer at index 7 for a total of three BST layers and then another at index 5, followed by another at index 12. Adding more BST layers lowers perplexity. However, the results seem to plateau at5 BST layers. We note also that there is a 3.5% training step time increase for each added layer. In Table 5, we train our models with different state sizes D. For the state size ablation, we use three BST layers at indices 0, 7, 9. We find that increasing D improves perplexity to the detriment of training speed (step time). For this reason, we chose D = 16 for Table 1 BST results. 16F JAX Implementation of BST Pseudocode 1 contains a function that implements convolution of multiple filters over the same input sequence using FFT and inverse FFT operations. Pseudocodes 2, 3 and 4 respectively implement context state collection of BST variants: Single-Head (SH), Multi-Head (MH) and Multi-Filter (MF). Finally, Pseudocode 5 runs the Block Transformer sublayer in parallel by feeding the context states to their corresponding block. """Unstructured filters and convolutions.""" import jax from jax import numpy as jnp from einops import rearrange win_length = 512 # (w) seq_length = 4096 # (l) def get_filters_unstruct(channels): """Returns trainable filters and biases. Args: channels: number of filters. Returns: h: filter of shape (seq_length, channels, dim) b: bias of shape (channels, dim) """ t = jnp.linspace(0.0, 1.0, seq_length) h = jnp.exp(- alpha * t) * dense(positional_emb(t)) b = get_bias() return h, b def multichannel_convolution(u, h, b): """Multichannel convolution function. Args: u: input of shape (seq_length, dim) h: filters of shape (seq_length, channels, dim) b: bias of shape (channels, dim) """ h = rearrange(h, "l c d -> c d l") fft_size = seq_length * 2 u_f = jnp.fft.rfft(x, n=fft_size) h_f = jnp.fft.rfft(h, n=fft_size) y = jnp.fft.irfft(h_f * x_f, n=fft_size, norm="forward")[ ..., :seq_length] # (c, d, l) y = y + x * b[..., None] # (c, d, l) y = rearrange(y, "c d l -> l d c") return y Pseudocode 1: Unstructured filters and convolutions. """Context state collection for BST-SH variant.""" num_heads = 8 # (h) num_states = 32 # (s) # (SH): Single-Head def SH_context_states(u): """Single-Head Context Collection.""" h, b = get_filters_[unstruct/s4](channels=1) 17y_1 = multichannel_convolution(u, h, b) # y_1: (l, d, 1) # lift to multiple heads y_h = dense(y_1) # y_h: (l, d, h) context_states = jnp.split( y_h, seq_length // win_length, axis=0) return context_states # (l/w, w, d, h) Pseudocode 2: Context state collection for BST-SH variants. """Context state collection for BST-MH variant.""" # (MH): Multi-Head def MH_context_states(u): """Multi-Head Context Collection.""" h, b = get_filters_[unstruct/s4](channels=num_heads) y_h = multichannel_convolution(u, h, b) # y_h: (l, d, h) context_states = jnp.split( y_h, seq_length // win_length, axis=0) return context_states # (l/w, w, d, h) Pseudocode 3: Context state collection for BST-MH variants. """Context state collection for BST-MF variant.""" # (MF): Multi-Filter def MF_context_states(u): """Multi-Filter Context Collection.""" h, b = get_filters_[unstruct/s4](channels=num_states) y_s = multichannel_convolution(u, h, b) # y_s: (l, d, s) context_states = jnp.split( y_s, seq_length // win_length, axis=0) # context_states: (l/w, w, d, s) # collect the last context states context_states = context_states[:, -1, ...] # (l/w, d, s) context_states = rearrange( context_states, "lw d s -> lw s d") # shift context states corresponding to windows context_states = jnp.roll(context_states, 1, axis=1) # replace the initial window with trainable weights init_context = get_init_context(num_states) # (d, s) context_states[0] = init_context # lift to multiple heads context_states = dense(context_states) return context_states # (l/w, s, d, h) Pseudocode 4: Context state collection for BST-MF variants. """Block-State Transformer Layer.""" # Block Transformers are non-recurrent and parallelizable. block_transformer = jax.vmap(BRecT.nonrecurrent_cell) def BST(u): """Block-State Transformer Layer.""" 18global MF # True if Multi-Filter, False otherwise (SH/MH) # split inputs into windows (l/w, w, d) u = jnp.split(u, seq_length // win_length, axis=0) # collect context states from SSM outputs context_states = [SH/MH/MF]_context_states(u) # pass the contexts in place of recurrent states y = block_transformer( token_embeddings=u, recurrent_state=context_states, use_cross_attn_causal_mask=not MF, use_cross_positional_emb=MF, # context IDs ) return rearrange(y, "lw w d -> (lw w) d") # (l, d) Pseudocode 5: Block-State Transformer Layer. 19

llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Introduces Block-State Transformer (BST), a fully-parallelizable hybrid layer that combines a State Space Model (SSM) for long-range contextualization with a Block Transformer for local modeling. Proposes three context-integration variants (Single-Head, Multi-Head, Multi-Filter). Achieves better or on-par language modeling perplexity than comparable Transformer-based architectures (e.g., BRECT, TRSF-XL) at matched compute, with up to >10× layer-level speedups vs Block-Recurrent Transformers under model parallelization. Demonstrates strong length generalization (up to 65k tokens) especially with structured SSMs (S4), and validates efficiency and effectiveness across PG19, arXiv, GitHub and LRA benchmarks. Shows that integrating SSM states into attention outperforms interleaving SSM and attention layers (e.g., GSS-HYBRID).
llm_extracted_info.methodology: BST layer pipeline: (1) An SSM sublayer processes the full sequence using convolution via FFT to produce a context-encoded sequence; both structured SSMs (S4) and unstructured, Hyena-style kernels are considered. (2) The sequence is partitioned into blocks (window length W). (3) For each block, token embeddings (queries) are cross-attended to SSM-derived context states plus local self-attention; the outputs are concatenated and projected. Three context-state constructions: SH (Single-Head): take the W most recent SSM states for the block and project to H heads; causal masking applied. MH (Multi-Head): SSM produces H head-specific outputs via separate C matrices, enabling complementary features; causal masking applied. MF (Multi-Filter): convolve with S distinct filters; stack the last state from the previous window (no causal mask needed); add learned context IDs. Positional handling: rely on SSM for positional information; use T5-style relative position bias; add context IDs in MF. Efficiency techniques: downsample inputs to 1/4 dimension before SSM to reduce FFTs; lift back before attention. Complexity: O(W^2) for block attention + O(L log L) for SSM convolution, versus O(LW) for BRECT. Implementation in JAX/Flax with provided pseudocode; ablations vary BST layer placement, number of BST layers, and SSM state size.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: Datasets: (1) PG19 (long English books; T5 32k vocab; test split). (2) arXiv (Math LaTeX subset; LaMDA 32k vocab; test split). (3) GitHub (code in C/C++/Java/Python/Go/TS; LaMDA 32k vocab; validation split). Models: ~200M and ~400M parameter regimes; window sizes 512 or 2048; 12 layers (small) or larger configs with more heads/MLP size; BST layers inserted at {1,7,9} (∼200M) and {1,5,7,9} (∼400M). Training: sequence length L=4k, batch size 32, Adam optimizer; training compute tracked in TPUv4 hours; JAX/Flax (Meliad). SSMs: structured S4 (N=16 state) and unstructured Hyena-style kernels; MF uses S=32 (small) or 64 (large) filters. Baselines: SLIDE:12L, Transformer-XL (TRSF-XL:2048), Block-Recurrent Transformer (BRECT:FIXED:SKIP), GSS-HYBRID-L. Metrics: Perplexity on each dataset; length generalization at seq lengths {512,16k,65k} after training at 4k; speed benchmarks on GPU (forward pass). Key results: For fixed compute, BST variants match or surpass BRECT and outperform GSS-HYBRID-L by ~1.5–4% across datasets; unstructured kernels often best at training length but weaker length generalization; BST:SH:S4-L generalizes best to 65k; BST layers are 6–11× faster than BRECT layers and near SLIDE speed; LRA classification: BST:SH:S4 (chunked) outperforms MEGA-CHUNK on 4/6 tasks and +1.5% average, though MEGA (unchunked) remains stronger overall. Ablations: more BST layers improve perplexity up to a plateau; larger SSM state D improves perplexity with slower step time; best single-layer placement around middle of the stack.
llm_extracted_info.limitations: FFT-based SSM sublayer is a speed bottleneck on TPUs (JAX FFT slower vs GPU); overall speed benefits rely on hardware parallelism. Unstructured kernels rely on learned positional encodings and degrade on unseen longer lengths; structured SSMs (S4) generalize better. Assumes SSM can compress long-term dependencies into limited states; a single vector per step may be insufficient for all dependencies. BST currently does not pass a non-differentiable cache across sequences, potentially losing context beyond training segment length L compared to BRECT. Evaluation primarily in LM and LRA; broader generalization to other long-range tasks not fully established. Training stability/hyperparameter tuning for hybrids not exhaustively explored; MF/SH/MH trade-offs depend on redundancy vs retrievability and may underutilize capacity.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: Integrate FFT-free or associative-scan SSMs (e.g., S5) or optimized I/O-aware FFTs for TPUs/GPUs. Add non-differentiable document-level memory/cache or retrieval (e.g., top-k memory like Memorizing Transformer) to preserve cross-segment context. Explore adaptive/context-aware selection of SSM states, larger block sizes, or hybrid routing between SH/MH/MF. Combine with stronger gating mechanisms (e.g., GAU/MEGA-style) or mixture-of-experts for scalability. Extend evaluation to more long-range reasoning/classification and multi-modal tasks; analyze robustness and stability at very long contexts. Study inference-time streaming variants and memory/computation trade-offs; optimize training curricula for length generalization. System-level co-design: fused kernels and layout for cross-attention to SSM contexts; broader hyperparameter sweeps and scaling laws for BST architectures.

research_study_list.title: State-space models with layer-wise nonlinearity are universal approximators with exponential decaying memory
research_study_list.abstract:
State-space models have gained popularity in sequence modelling due to their
simple and efficient network structures. However, the absence of nonlinear
activation along the temporal direction limits the model's capacity. In this
paper, we prove that stacking state-space models with layer-wise nonlinear
activation is sufficient to approximate any continuous sequence-to-sequence
relationship. Our findings demonstrate that the addition of layer-wise
nonlinear activation enhances the model's capacity to learn complex sequence
patterns. Meanwhile, it can be seen both theoretically and empirically that the
state-space models do not fundamentally resolve the issue of exponential
decaying memory. Theoretical results are justified by numerical verifications.
research_study_list.full_text: State-space Models with Layer-wise Nonlinearity are Universal Approximators with Exponential Decaying Memory Shida Wang Department of Mathematics National University of Singapore e0622338@u.nus.edu Beichen Xue Department of Statistics and Data Science National University of Singapore e0773769@u.nus.edu Abstract State-space models have gained popularity in sequence modelling due to their simple and efficient network structures. However, the absence of nonlinear ac- tivation along the temporal direction limits the model’s capacity. In this paper, we prove that stacking state-space models with layer-wise nonlinear activation is sufficient to approximate any continuous sequence-to-sequence relationship. Our findings demonstrate that the addition of layer-wise nonlinear activation enhances the model’s capacity to learn complex sequence patterns. Meanwhile, it can be seen both theoretically and empirically that the state-space models do not fundamentally resolve the issue of exponential decaying memory. Theoretical results are justified by numerical verifications. 1 Introduction State-space model [ 1–5] is an emerging family of neural networks specialized in learning long sequence relationships. It achieves significantly better performance compared with attention-based transformers in the long range arena (LRA) dataset [ 6, 7]. Despite its effectiveness, the state- space model is built on a relatively simple foundation of linear-RNN-like layers. One of the key advantages of state-space models is their simple recurrence, which enables efficient acceleration. In fact, this recurrence allows for an asymptotic computational complexity of onlyO(T log T), which is significantly better than the O(T2) complexity of traditional full-attention approaches [8]. A natural question would be whether SSM achieves this speedup with certain sacrifices in model capacity or memory property. It is currently unclear whether the state-space model’s linear architecture with layerwise nonlinearity possesses sufficient expressive capacity to approximate any target sequence-to- sequence relationship. This knowledge would be important to answer pertinent questions regarding the model’s ability to handle the complexity of real-world datasets characterized by diverse and intricate sequence relationships. In particular, considering the speed advantage of SSM over attention- based transformers, the universal approximation property impacts whether a state-space model could be a suitable replacement for a transformer. In this paper, we study the universality of state-space model. Furthermore, the memory property is investigated and we show that state-space models also have an asymptotically exponential decaying memory. The main contributions can be summarized as follow 1. We give a constructive proof for the universal approximation property for multi-layer state- space models. The width dependency on sequence length is analyzed. 37th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2023). arXiv:2309.13414v3  [cs.LG]  1 Nov 2023... ... Temporal direction Depth direction Layer-wise nonlinearity recurrent activation Figure 1: Network structure of two-layer state-space model. 2. The state-space models are shown to have an exponentially decaying memory, which coincides with usual recurrent neural networks. 3. Numerical verifications for the memory property are given on synthetic datasets. 2 Background In this section, we introduce the general form of the state-space models. The classical results on universal approximation for recurrent neural networks are summarized. Based on the approximation result, the well-documented challenge of learning long-term memory via recurrent networks is summarized. In particular, we give the definition of memory function which we shall use in the derivation of memory decay. 2.1 State-space models Single-layer state-space model can be viewed as a recurrent neural network without nonlinear recurrent activation. As is shown in Figure 1, the discrete time version of SSM [9] is hk+1 = W hk + Uxk+1, h 0 = 0 (1) yk = Chk + Dxk = CW kh0 + kX i=1 CW k−iUxi + Dxk. (2) where x ∈ Rdin , y ∈ Rdout , h ∈ Rm, W ∈ Rm×m, U ∈ Rm×din , C ∈ Rdout×m, D ∈ Rdout×din . Notice that we drop the bias term for simplicity. For multi-layer state-space model, the nonlinear activation is added layer-wise. The continuous time version of a single layer is dht dt = W ht + Uxt, h 0 = 0 (3) yt = Cht + Dxt = Z T 0 CeW(t−s)Uxsds + Dxt. (4) It can be seen in Equation (4) that the first component of output y is the convolution between kernel function ρ(t) = CeW(t−s)U and x. In discrete case, the convolution operator ∗ is represented by y[0,T] = ρ(t) ∗ x[0,T] ⇒ yk = kX i=0 ρk−ixi. (5) Compared with temporal convolution network (TCN) [10], which has a finite kernel size, state-space models can be regarded as implementing global convolution (ρ(t) = CeW(t−s)U, t≥ 0) over the 2temporal axis. Numerically, since the kernel size is the same as the sequence length, the convolution implemented via fast Fourier transform can lead to significant accelerations [11]. Compared with attention-based transformer, it is shown that SSM can speed up the training 100x in learning sequences with length 64K [12]. The state-space model’s temporal efficiency is achieved by eliminating the nonlinear activation function in its recurrent layers hk+1 = W hk + Uxk + b, resulting in faster processing of sequential data via parallel scan [11]. 2.2 Universal approximation in RNN It is long-known that recurrent neural networks with nonlinear sigmoidal activations (such as tanh and sigmoid) are universal approximators. Theorem 2.1 (Simplified universality statement). For any sequence to sequence map: H : x → y and tolerance ϵ, there exists a hidden dimension m and weights c, W, U, bsuch that the RNN with weights (c, W, U, b) can approximate the target sequence to sequence map: ∥y − ˆy∥ ≤ϵ. (6) where prediction sequence ˆy is given by hk+1 = σ(W hk + Uxk + b), (7) ˆyk = C⊤hk. (8) Universal approximation establishes the feasibility of learning sequence to sequence relationships via recurrent neural networks. However, typical approximation rate results depend on the sequence length [13]. Sequence length dependent approximation rate does not generalize to the case of sequence of infinite length. In learning sequences with infinite length, Li et al. [14] shows that linear RNNs have difficulty in learning non-exponential decaying memory. Various numerical experiments [15] confirm that adding nonlinear recurrent activation does not fundamentally change the decay. In state-space models, the nonlinearity is included in a layer-wise approach. It is unknown whether such layer-wise nonlinearity alone is sufficient to approximate any sequence to sequence relationships. 2.3 Memory function and curse of memory In this paper we study the memory property of state-space model. Before we introduce the main results, we present a simple memory function definition in sequence modelling. Li et al. [14] proves that a bounded causal continuous regular time-homogeneous linear functional has the following Riesz representation: yt = Ht(x) = Z t −∞ ρ(t − s)xsds. (9) Here ρ : R+ → R is an L1-integrable function. If ρt rapidly decreases with t, then the target sequence relationship has a short-term memory. Since ρt fully captures the memory property of a linear functional [ 14], we call it the memory function (of the linear functional). In particular, when approximating the linear functional with linear RNNs, the model’s memory function is ˆρ(t − s) = C⊤eW(t−s)U. The curse of memory refers to the phenomenon that if a target linear functional can be approximated by a sequence of linear RNNs, the memory function ρt decays exponentially. Take the test input to be xtest = 1 t ≥ 0, 0 t <0. Notice that the derivative of the linear functional at test input extracts the memory function    d dt Ht(xtest)    = |ρ(t)|2. Therefore a natural extension of the memory function [16] to the bounded causal continuous regular time-homogeneous nonlinear functionals will be: ˆρ(t) =      dˆyt dt      2 , ˆyt = bHt(xtest). (10) It can be seen this definition is compatible with the memory function definition of linear functional. Also, this memory function can be evaluated by computing the models’ derivative at the test input using finite difference method. 3...  ...  Figure 2: Two-layer state-space model can approximate any continuous element-wise function 3 Main results In this section, we first give a simple constructive proof to show that any element-wise function on input sequence can be approximated by a two-layer state-space model. Next, we show any temporal convolution can be approximated by a state-space model. The constructive proof for general nonlinear sequence to sequence functional is given based on the above propositions. Moreover, as the Kolmogorov-Arnold-representation-based construction has a weight number dependent on sequence length, it can be highly inefficient to construct a shallow wide network to learn long sequences. To reduce the widths dependency on the sequence length, a V olterra-series-based construction is demonstrated. 3.1 Two-layer SSM approximates element-wise function By element-wise function, we mean learning sequence relationship with form (x 1, . . . , x T ) → (f (x 1), . . . , f (x T )). Proposition 3.1. For any given continuous function f over a compact set K , let the activation σ be sigmoidal function. lim z→∞ σ (z ) = 1, lim z→−∞ σ (z ) = −1. (15) The proof is included in Appendix B.2. The main idea is to approximate element-wise function f (x ) with U 2σ (U 1x + b 1). A graphical demonstration is given in Figure 2. It can be seen the two-layer state-space model can approximate any element-wise function by setting W 1 = W 2 = b 2 = 0. 3.2 SSM approximates temporal convolution In this part we show that state-space models can approximate temporal convolution: y k =Pk i=1 ρ k−ix i, 1 ≤ k ≤ T. According to Equation (3), the hidden state of SSM is the con- volution between input sequence and exponentially decaying function ˆρ k = CW kU . y k =Pk i=1 CW k−iUx i + CW kh 0 + Dx k. 4Therefore the approximation problem of temporal convolution by state-space models is reduced to the approximation problem of general convolution kernel ρk, 1 ≤ k ≤ T by exponentially decaying convolution kernel ˆρk, 1 ≤ k ≤ T. The optimal weights are defined by C, W, U= arg minC,W,U supk |ρk − CW kU|. Proposition 3.2. For any given convolution kernelρk, 1 ≤ k ≤ T, the single-layer state-space model is universal approximator for temporal convolution. In other words, for any ϵ >0, there exists a hidden dimension m and corresponding weights C, W, U such that ρk = CW kU satisfies sup k |ρk − ˆρk| < ϵ. (16) See Appendix B.3 for the proof. The main idea is to represent the single-layer state-space model output as a convolution between input and kernel function. The approximation of temporal convolution is then reduced to the approximation of general kernel with the SSM-induced kernels. Remark 3.3. Although the Proposition 3.2 indicates that we can use single-layer state-space model to approximate any convolution, it does not reveal the necessary hidden dimension m for such approximation. 3.3 Universality of SSM Now we show that five-layer state-space model is universal. Without loss of generality, assume the output y is one-dimensional. The main proof is based on the famous Kolmogorov-Arnold repre- sentation theorem [17]. By Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem, we know any multivariate continuous function f : Rd → R can be represented by f(x1, . . . , xd) = 2dX q=0 Φq  dX p=1 ϕq,p(xp) ! . (17) Remark 3.4. George Lorentz [ 18] shows that we can use the same Φ: f(x1, . . . , xd) =P2d q=0 Φ Pd p=1 ϕq,p(xp)  . Braun and Griebel [20] gives the first con- structive proof for the superposition. Moreover, the inner function ϕ is shown to be independent of target function f. It means the learning of function ϕ can be approximated without retraining for different target functionals. We summarize the Kolmogorov-Arnold theorem as follow: Theorem 3.5 ([17, 20]). Fix dimension d ≥ 2. There are real numbers a, bp, cq and a continuous and monotone function ϕ : R → R, such that for any continuous function f : [0, 1]d → R, there exists a continuous function Φ : R → R with f(x1, . . . , xd) = 2dX q=0 Φ  dX p=1 bpϕ(xp + qa) + cq ! . Similar approaches have been adopted by idea of implicit convolution in CKConv [22]. 3.4 Memory decay of SSM In the ensuing discourse, we turn our focus towards an examination of the memory property inherent in state-space models. It has been thoroughly studied in literature that recurrent neural networks exhibit a phenomenon of exponential memory decay [ 14]. An intriguing question that naturally arises in this context is whether state-space models are plagued by similar challenges. Upon careful investigation, it is concluded that state-space models, much like their neural network counterparts, do possess an asymptotically exponential decaying memory. Proposition 3.11. (26) 4 Numerical verifications Based on the generalization of memory function ρ in linear functional, we verify the asymptotic memory decay of state-space models with simple randomly generated models. The definition is given in one-dimensional case, but it can be generalized to multi-variable case by taking different unit inputs in various coordinates. The motivation for the above definition is based on the idea of measuring the earlier input at the later output. In our experiment, we construct various RNN models and SSM with random generated weights. It can be seen in Figure 5 that the memory of naive state-space models also has an exponentially decay memory. It is consistent with the previous theorem that state-space model has an asymptotic exponentially decaying memory. Notice that here the naive SSM is simply adding a tanh activation across layers without specially tuning the weights. Such random initialization can expose the memory issue more significantly as the S4 layer is constructed with several parameterization techniques. However, the manually constructed S4 still has an asymptotic exponential decaying memory as is shown in Figure 6. 5 Related Work In this section, we introduce the previous works on state-space models. As the single-layer state- space model is a linear RNN, we summarize the related approximation work on RNN. In particular, the approximation result and memory result is emphasized as this paper works on the universal approximation property and memory decay property of SSM. State-space models State-space models originate from the HIPPO matrix which is optimal in the online function approximation sense [2–4]. The Hippo matrix initialization for recurrent matrix W enables the state-space model to have a slow decaying memory. The universal approximation idea is heuristically demonstrated in Orvieto et al. [23]. However, the proof from the Koopman theory perspective only guarantees the existence of universal approximation. Our proof is a constructive proof which can be further generalized to study the approximation rate with respect to the hidden dimensions and network depths. Recurrent neural networks Recurrent neural networks (RNNs) [24] are one of the most popular neural networks for sequence modelling. Various results have been established in RNNs approx- imation theory, see Sontag [25], Hanson et al. [13]. Apart from the universal approximation, the exponential decaying memory property is the notorious phenomenon in recurrent neural networks which prohibits the scale up of the models in terms of the sequence length [14, 26]. 6 Discussion In Table 1 we compare the classical sequence models including RNN, TCN and attention-based transformer. The state-space model can be considered as an enhancement of Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) due to its superior optimization and inference speed. Despite maintaining a similar network topology, inference cost, and memory pattern, it provides a more efficient and streamlined approach. The effort to extend the long-memory learning is also carried out in convolutional networks and attention-based transformers. Romero et al. It is shown that the nonlinear recurrent activations in classical recurrent neural networks are not necessary when there are nonlinear activations across different hidden layers. This result implies state-space model is as powerful as the classical recurrent neural networks in the approximation sense. Furthermore, we study the memory decay in multi-layer state-space models, which is a notorious issue in classical recurrent neural networks. While empirical evidence suggests 90 500 1000 1500 2000 time t 10 8 10 7 10 6 10 5 10 4 10 3 10 2 10 1 memory  layers=1 layers=2 layers=3 Figure 6: Memory function of a randomly initialized S4. For each model, as the time increases, the memory function can be “capped” by a straight line, which indicate that the memories are decaying exponentially. Our research has exciting implications for future work in state-space models. By extending our work to the approximation rate of state-space models, we can obtain better understanding of state-space models’ hypothesis space. Such result is important to further optimize the architecture in various real-world applications. The multi-layer linear Recurrent Neural Network (RNN), including a bias term, can be perceived as a linear combination of various depth-specific linear RNNs, devoid of a bias term. 13B Proof B.1 Universal approximation Here we include the universal approximation from Barron [27], it is frequently used in the later constructive proof for state-space models. Theorem B.1 (Cybenko [28], Barron [27]). For any continous function f : Rd → R and compact set K ⊆ Rd. Assume the activation function σ : R → R is bounded and sigmoidal: lim z→∞ σ(z) = 1, lim z→−∞ σ(z) = −1. (41) For any ϵ >0, there exists m ∈ N, A∈ Rm×d, b∈ Rk, C∈ Rm×k such that sup x∈K ∥f(x) − g(x)∥ ≤ϵ, (42) where g(x) = Cσ(Ax + b). (43) B.2 Proof for Proposition 3.1 Proof. Fix the bounded continuous function f. For any ϵ > 0, according to the universal approximation theorem in Theorem B.1, there exists U1, U2, b1 such that |f(x) − U2σ(U1x + b1)| ≤ϵ. (44) Take W1 = W2 = b2 = 0, the two-layer state-space model can approximate element-wise continuous functions. (Here the element-wise function mean the sequences that for each k, yk only depends on xk.) B.3 Proof for Proposition 3.2 Proof. The original statement is given in the discrete index. We prove the result for the continuous index and the discrete case can be derived by discretization. Without loss of generality, assume the input output sequence are all one-dimensional. Otherwise we shall stacking the C, W, Ufor different input-output channels. Since the input and output are one-dimensional, we know c, uare vectors and W is a square matrix. To approximate the target convolution: yt = Z t 0 ρt−sxsds (45) by state-space model ˆyt = Z t 0 c⊤eW(t−s)uxsds. (46) Since the prediction error can be decomposed into following form yt − ˆyt = Z t 0  ρt−s − c⊤eW(t−s)u  xsds. (47) Approximating the temporal convolution with state-space model over all bounded inputsx can be reduced to function approximating problem: max s∈[0,T]      ρs − mX i=1 cie−λis       < ϵ, λi ≥ 0. (48) In other words, approximating a convolution layer with state-space model is equivalent to approxi- mating a general integrable function by function with exponential form ˆρs = Pm i=1 cie−λis. 14By change of variable, we have max τ∈[e−T ,1] |f(τ) − mX i=1 ciτλi | < ϵ. (49) Here f(τ) = ρ−log(τ). Since polynomials are universal approximators on compact intervals, we know there exists ci, λi such that the aforementioned inequality is satisfied. (For example, if f is smooth, we can take the Taylor expansion of function f.) B.4 Proof for Proposition 3.6 Proof. Our proof is based on Equation (18), for any sequence relationship H : {xk}T k=1 → {yk}T k=1, we need to approximate ϕ and Φ, and subsequently approximate the representation prescribed by the Kolmogorov-Arnold theorem. Fix tolerance ϵ. As is shown in Figure 3, the first element-wise function ϕ(·) can be approximated by two-layer state-space model. This is a result from the direct application of Proposition 3.1. In math terms, there exists U1, U2, b1 such that two-layer state-space model approximate function ϕ. ∥ϕ(x) − U2σ(U1x + b1)∥ ≤ϵ. (50) We shall denote U2σ(U1x + b1) by ˆϕ(x). Next, according to Proposition 3.2, we know the (temporal) convolution can be approximated via single-layer state-space model. (Figure 3) Φ( TX p=1 C3WT−p 3 U3 ˆϕ(xp + qa)) − U5(U4( TX p=1 C3WT−p 3 U3 ˆϕ(xp + qa)) + b4)  ≤ ϵ. (52) Based on the above result, without loss of generality we assume the outer function Φ is Lipschitz continuous with coefficient L, then the final approximation error is bounded by L∥ρ∥L1 ϵ + Lϵ + ϵ. Φ( TX p=1 ρT−pϕ(xi + qa)) − U5(U4( TX p=1 C3WT−p 3 U3 ˆϕ(xp + qa)) + b4)  (53) ≤ Φ( TX p=1 ρT−pϕ(xi + qa)) − Φ( TX p=1 ρT−p ˆϕ(xp + qa))  (54) + Φ( TX p=1 ρT−p ˆϕ(xp + qa)) − Φ( TX p=1 C3WT−p 3 U3 ˆϕ(xp + qa))  (55) + Φ( TX p=1 C3WT−p 3 U3 ˆϕ(xp + qa)) − U5(U4( TX p=1 C3WT−p 3 U3 ˆϕ(xp + qa)) + b4)  (56) ≤L∥ρ∥L1 ϵ + Lϵ + ϵ. (57) To summarize, we achieve the approximation of general nonlinear sequence-to-sequence relationship with representation (with Lipschitz continuous Φ) via five-layer state-space model. Since the Lipscthiz continuous function is dense in the set of continuous function, therefore the universal approximation can be generalized to the representation with Φ not necessarily Lipschitz continuous. 15B.5 Proof for Proposition 3.9 Proof. For simplicity, we will only present the approximation of an n-th order component of the V olterra Series. The general approximation of nonlinear sequence-to-sequence relationship can be achieved by approximating different order component separately and taking the linear combination. Consider the target functional yn(t) = Z t 0 ··· Z t 0 hn(τ1, . . . , τn) nY j=1 x(t − τj)dτj. (58) The state-space model’s n-th order term can be represented by ˆyn(t) = mX i=1 Z t 0 ··· Z t 0   nY j=1 ˆh(m) j (τj)   nY j=1 x(t − τj)dτj. (59) It can be seen the kernel function of state-space model is ˆhn(τ1, . . . , τn) = Pm i=1 Qn j=1 ˆh(m) j (τj) while the original n-th order kernel is multi-variable function hn(τ1, . . . , τn). For any tolerance ϵ, there exists a sufficiently large hidden dimension m such that the multi-variable function is approximated by the single-variable function’s product:        mX i=1 nY j=1 ˆh(m) j (τj) − hn(τ1, . . . , τn)        ≤ ϵ. (60) For example, we may consider ˆhn to be polynomial of τj and take the Taylor expansion of kernel hn. Remark B.2. While the aforementioned proof for approximation is provided through a polynomial method, it’s commonly accepted that polynomials may not be the most efficient means to parameterize kernel functions. Consequently, a variety of techniques have been empirically investigated to represent convolutional layers [22]. B.6 Proof for Proposition 3.11 Proof. As xt converges to x∗ exponentially and ρ is integrable, we know the limit of yt exists lim t→∞ yt = lim t→∞ Z ∞ 0 ρsxt−sds = Z ∞ 0 ρs lim t→∞ xt−sds = Z ∞ 0 ρsx∗ds. (61) Here ρs = C⊤eWs U, s≥ 0 is the memory function for (continuous) state-space model. By the dominated convergence theorem, asρ is integrable and ec0t|xt−s −x∗| is a bounded sequence, lim t→∞ ec0t∥yt − y∗∥ = lim t→∞ ec0t  Z ∞ 0 ρs(xt−s − x∗)ds  (62) ≤  Z ∞ 0 |ρs| lim t→∞ ec0t|xt−s − x∗|ds  (63) =  Z ∞ 0 |ρs| ·0ds  = 0. (64) B.7 Proof for Proposition 3.12 Proof. Since limt→∞ xt = x∗, by continuity of the activation function σ(·) lim t→∞ yt = lim t→∞ σ(xt) = σ( lim t→∞ xt) = σ(x∗). (65) Hereafter we define y∗ = σ(x∗). 16Since σ is Lipschitz continuous, let L be its Lipschitz constant lim t→∞ ec0t∥yt − y∗∥ = lim t→∞ ec0t∥σ(xt) − σ(x∗)∥ (66) ≤ lim t→∞ ec0tL∥xt − x∗∥ (67) = L lim t→∞ ec0t∥xt − x∗∥ (68) = 0. (69) The Lipschitz continuity is a weak assumption that most of the activations such as ReLU, GeLU, tanh, hardtanh satisfy. C Limitations Theoretical results on approximation do not have quantitative results. Therefore it is not easy to directly compare the efficiency of different types of models over a specific task. The good news is that the general memory function servers as a guideline in constructing models. Take the Hyena [12] architecture as an example, as the implicit representation of convolution kernel is utilized in the network, Hyena is not a recurrent model. If we replace the convolution layer by state-space model, we can “translate” a non-recurrent neural network into a recurrent neural network without sacrificing the approximation capacity. The main advantage of recurrent networks, compared with implicit convolution form, lies in the lower inference memory cost. This study primarily explores the qualitative attributes of the state space model within the context of approximation theory. To better delineate it from other architectures like linear transformers, a more in-depth investigation into the rate of approximation is necessary. D Comparisons between RNNs, SSMs and S4 In Table 2, we compare different recurrent models in terms of the recurrence, universality, temporal parallel and the memory decay speed. Our core findings are established for SSM; however, we argue that they are applicable to S4 as well. The primary differences between the vanilla state-space models (SSMs) and S4 involve model parameterisation, weight initialisation, discretisation, normalisation, dropout, and residual connections. The model architectures of SSMs and S4 are almost identical, as both alternately stack linear RNNs and nonlinear activation layers. Therefore, in terms of universal approximation, the approximation capacities of both SSMs and S4 are equivalent. Linear RNN Nonlinear RNN SSMs S4 Recurrence Yes Yes Yes Yes Universality No Yes (hardtanh, tanh) Yes (ours) Yes (ours) Temporal Parallel Yes No Yes Yes Exponential decay Yes [14] Yes [16] Yes (ours) Yes, moderated (ours) Table 2: Comparisons between RNNs, SSMs and S4 E Further discussion on the two proofs for universality of SSMs We provided comparison between the two proof methods: An analogy between Kolmogorov-theorem and classical fully-connected neural networks can be drawn because of the finite number of layers in both. It is shown in Eldan and Shamir [29] that a simple function expressible by a small 3-layer feed- forward neural networks cannot be approximated to a certain accuracy unless the network’s width is exponential in the dimension. In contrast, V olterra-Series shares similarities with deep learning, and as a result, the advantages of deep learning over classical fully-connected neural networks carry over 17to this approach. The authors are inclined to consider V olterra-Series based construction as relatively superior, and increasing the depth to be a more efficient way to scale up the model. 18

llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Provides constructive proofs that multi-layer state-space models (SSMs) with layer-wise nonlinear activations are universal approximators for bounded, causal, time-homogeneous continuous sequence-to-sequence mappings. Establishes: (1) two-layer SSMs approximate any element-wise function; (2) single-layer SSMs approximate any temporal convolution; (3) five-layer SSMs are universal via Kolmogorov–Arnold superposition; and (4) a Volterra-series-based construction yielding sequence-length–independent widths. Shows theoretically and empirically that SSMs exhibit asymptotically exponential decaying memory, similar to RNNs, including for S4 (though decay is slower).
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Theoretical constructions: (a) Use Cybenko/Barron universal approximation to show two-layer SSMs approximate element-wise nonlinearities; (b) Show single-layer SSM outputs are global convolutions with kernels of the form C W^k U and approximate arbitrary convolution kernels via sums of exponentials; (c) Combine with Kolmogorov–Arnold representation f(x)=Σq Φ(Σp bp ϕ(xp+qa)+cq) to build a five-layer SSM: two layers for ϕ, one SSM for temporal convolution, two layers for Φ. 
- Alternative universality via Volterra series: approximate nth-order kernels hn(τ1,...,τn) using low-rank tensor products of 1D SSM-induced kernels, achieving widths not explicitly dependent on sequence length.
- Memory analysis: define a nonlinear memory function via model derivative at a step input; prove exponential decay under stability (|eig(W)|≤1) and Lipschitz layer-wise activations by induction across layers; provide derivations for linear and multi-layer cases.
- Empirical verification: estimate memory functions via finite differences at step input and observe decay curves.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: Synthetic validations only. Models: vanilla RNN, GRU, LSTM, naive multi-layer SSMs (with tanh across layers), and S4. Weights randomly initialized; no training. Input: step function (xtest(t)=1 for t≥0, 0 otherwise). Metric: memory function estimated via finite differences of output derivative at the test input. Protocol: vary number of layers (1–4); repeat 100 times to report error bars; sequence horizons up to ~120 (RNN/GRU/LSTM/naive SSM) and up to ~2000 (S4). Outcome: all models show exponential memory decay; S4 decays more slowly but still exponentially. No real datasets or standard benchmarks used.
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- No quantitative approximation rates or finite-sample bounds; universality is qualitative. 
- Kolmogorov-based construction leads to width scaling linearly with sequence length; efficiency may be poor for long sequences. 
- Proposition on convolution universality lacks explicit bounds on required hidden dimension m. 
- Assumptions: bounded, causal, time-homogeneous continuous functionals; stability (spectral radius ≤1) and Lipschitz layer-wise activations. 
- Empirical evidence limited to synthetic settings with random weights; no trained models or real-world benchmarks; thus practical effectiveness and training dynamics are not evaluated. 
- Exponential memory decay persists asymptotically, implying inherent limitations for very long-term dependencies.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Derive approximation rates and explicit width–depth trade-offs; provide bounds on hidden dimension for kernel/convolution approximation. 
- Architectures or mechanisms to mitigate exponential memory decay (e.g., multi-timescale states, gating, learned long kernels, residual/skip connections, hybrids with attention or implicit convolutions like CKConv/Hyena). 
- Improved parameterizations/initializations (e.g., HIPPO variants) to slow decay while preserving stability; study stability–capacity trade-offs. 
- Empirical validation on real long-context benchmarks (e.g., LRA, long-context language modeling) with trained SSMs; compare against transformers and hybrid models. 
- Extend theory beyond time-homogeneous/causal settings, to non-stationary inputs and non-Lipschitz components; analyze robustness and generalization. 
- Investigate low-rank/tensor decompositions for higher-order Volterra kernels and efficient implementations with FFT/parallel scan; explore depth-vs-width efficiency systematically.

new_method.method:
Refined research idea: LAMBS++ — a length‑aware, budgeted router and scheduler for per‑token/head hybridization of SSM/attention/retrieval with discrete, hardware‑aligned kernels

Motivation
- Problem: The optimal mix of SSM and attention varies at fine granularity (token, head, position) and is heavily constrained by device‑specific bottlenecks (p95 latency, memory bandwidth, energy), not FLOPs. Production inputs are heterogeneous in length and structure (short chat vs. long docs), and operators need predictable SLOs on diverse GPU/TPU SKUs.
- Gaps in prior work:
  • BST and other hybrids choose mixing statically at layer or block level without online, per‑token/head branching or kernel selection; they optimize FLOPs or average latency, not tail behavior; and they lack a runtime that converts routing choices into actual wall‑clock gains.
  • H3/Mamba/DeltaNet and their hybrids provide efficient SSMs but use static placement; no device‑calibrated, tail‑aware routing/kernels; limited safeguards for length extrapolation when unstructured filters drift.
  • Distillation/speculative decoding works (e.g., Mamba-in-the-Llama) improve speed by architectural replacement and decoding kernels, not by per‑token routing under device budgets.
- Trend: Deployed LLMs face strict SLOs, rising energy/BW constraints, and increasingly long/heterogeneous context distributions. A systems–model co‑design is needed to make token/head‑level routing decisions that directly target p95/p99 latency/BW/energy while preserving quality.

Methodology
Core idea: LAMBS++ adds a length‑aware, multi‑objective, budgeted router (per token/head) that selects among attention/SSM/retrieval branches and a discrete set of hardware‑aligned kernel SKUs, coupled to a speculative scheduler that issues only the chosen kernels for the chosen tokens/heads. It is a drop‑in wrapper for JAX/Flax BST‑style stacks and PyTorch Mamba/DeltaNet hybrids.

1) Hybrid block with discrete SKUs (hardware‑aligned)
- Branches: sliding/block local attention; structured SSM scans (S4D/S5/DeltaNet/Mamba‑like); optional retrieval. Outputs are fused by per‑head gated additive mixing y_h = g_attn·y_attn + g_ssm·y_ssm (+ g_ret·y_ret), Σg=1.
- Discrete SKUs: attention windows W∈{256,512,1024,2048}; SSM state/timescale packs S∈{8,16,32,64}; retrieval depth k∈{0,1,2,4,8}; optional head dropping and long‑row sparsity SKUs. Each SSM SKU includes multi‑timescale decays and eigenvalue regularizers for stability.
- Novelty: Unlike static hybrids, per‑token/head branch+SKU is chosen online to satisfy device budgets.

2) Hierarchical router R with low‑cost taps and discrete choices
- Two‑stage routing to preserve kernel efficiency:
  • Stage A (coarse): per‑block or per‑layer group router selects a small SKU subset per head‑group to keep tiles hot.
  • Stage B (fine): per‑token/head gates g and categorical SKU indices within the chosen subset.
- Inputs (cheap, pre‑heavy‑ops): top‑1 QK and small‑window entropy; ANN distances to recent keys; SSM running variance; relative position; sequence length hint; rarity hashes; a hysteresis state from prior router logits; a tiny uncertainty head (MC‑dropout or low‑rank ensemble variance).
- Outputs: categorical {branch, SKU} per token/head, optional head‑drop, and a one‑step predict‑ahead R_{t+1} for scheduling.
- Training discretization/regularizers: straight‑through Gumbel for categorical SKUs; fragmentation loss to encourage contiguous run‑length segments; group‑wise routing to keep matmul/scan tiles warm; distance prior (near→attention, far→SSM/RAG); uncertainty‑biased routing toward more stable branches when ambiguous.
- Novelty vs prior gating: discrete SKU choice at token/head granularity with hierarchical routing explicitly shaped for kernel efficiency.

3) Multi‑objective training with device‑calibrated costs and tail control
- Loss: L = task_loss + Σ λ_i·C_i + β·KL(g_prior||g) + γ·Redundancy(y_attn,y_ssm) + ρ·Fragmentation.
- Costs C_i from live micro‑profiled LUTs: attention(W), SSM(S), retrieval(k), KV‑cache traffic, peak smem/register footprint, and energy/token (NVML/TPU telemetry). LUTs are indexed by (SKU, shape, dtype, device, batch layout) and refreshed online.
- Tail latency: minimize CVaR_α (α≈0.95) of per‑sequence latency; adapt λ_CVaR via a PI controller to track SLO (p95 ms/token and/or joules/token). This explicitly optimizes tail behavior that FLOPs proxies miss.
- Training schedule:
  • Phase 1 explore: λ≈0, entropy bonus; teacher g_prior from a full‑attention + long‑SSM teacher; establish initial LUTs.
  • Phase 2 tighten: enable discrete SKUs, fragmentation penalty, predict‑ahead; Lagrangian dual ascent on λ to satisfy p95/BW/energy targets; periodically reprofile LUTs and update device fingerprints.
  • Optional Phase 3 constrained RL: treat router as a policy under latency/energy constraints with reward = −task_loss − κ·max(0, latency−SLO) − η·energy.
- Novelty: device‑calibrated LUTs with CVaR tail control and live updates; prior works optimize FLOPs/means and assume static kernels.

4) Speculative scheduling runtime: predict‑then‑execute
- Use R_{t+1} to bucket tokens/heads by (branch, SKU), issue only required kernels, and fuse small buckets; masks are run‑length‑encoded and head‑grouped to reduce divergence.
- CUDA Graphs/Triton/Flash hooks are pre‑captured per (branch,SKU,shape) to stabilize launch overhead; TPU path uses associative/scan kernels.
- Misprediction repair: fast discrepancy checks trigger a cheap fallback kernel on the affected subset; router EMA state updated.
- Decode support: streaming SSM scans; cache‑aware windowed attention; router+scheduler overhead <1–2% of block time.
- Novelty: converts routing into wall‑clock wins; prior dynamic methods typically lack a scheduler or repair path.

5) Length‑generalization safeguards
- Route far‑range to structured SSM/retrieval; cap unstructured filters to near‑range via a learned max‑S_choice.
- Spectral regularization (eigenvalue clipping), multi‑timescale SKU packs, and a length curriculum (e.g., 4k→32k→128k) for robust extrapolation.

6) Integration plan (code‑level)
- Shared modules: RouterR (hierarchical), DiscreteSKUManager, CostProfiler (micro‑profiler + LUTs), KernelScheduler, UncertaintyAdapter, BudgetController.
- JAX/Flax (BST‑style): add SSMContextScan (S4D/S5/DeltaNet) with top‑k state pruning; replace concat mixing by per‑head gated‑add; plumb W/S/k SKUs via transformer_layer APIs; TPU associative‑scan; GPU FFT path retained when beneficial.
- PyTorch (Mamba/DeltaNet hybrids): wrap each block with parallel MHA and Mamba/DeltaNet branches; mask matmul/scan per token/head; grouped execution via Triton/Flash with SKU‑specific kernels; speculative scheduler at wrapper level. Optional synergy with INT4: expose separate LUT rows for INT4/FP8/FP16.

7) Evaluation
- Benchmarks: PG19, arXiv‑Math, GitHub code, LRA; long‑context RAG/QA; real deployment trace replay for latency/energy (mixed prompt lengths and batch shapes).
- Metrics: PPL/accuracy; p50/p95/p99 latency; energy/token; memory BW; misprediction/fallback rate; LUT drift; router overhead.
- Ablations: static hybrids vs LAMBS++; gating w/o SKU choice; SKU choice w/o scheduler; retrieval on/off; fragmentation penalty on/off; hierarchical vs flat router; CVaR vs mean latency; with/without uncertainty bias.

How this differs from prior work
- BST: we learn per‑token/head branch and discrete SKU under device budgets and provide a scheduler that turns routing into wall‑clock gains; BST is static and FLOPs‑oriented.
- H3/Mamba/DeltaNet hybrids: efficient SSMs but static placement; no per‑token/head SKU choice, no device‑calibrated tail objective, no runtime scheduler.
- Distillation/speculative decoding (Mamba‑in‑the‑Llama): focuses on replacing layers and decoding kernels; LAMBS++ retains both branches and learns when/how/which kernel to run online with SLO tracking.
- INT4 training, OT fusion, SlotSSM: orthogonal to budgeted routing/scheduling; none optimize p95 with discrete, hardware‑aligned SKU selection at token/head granularity.

Expected impact
- Quality–efficiency Pareto: For the same p95 latency/BW/energy, LAMBS++ deploys attention where near‑range matching is predictive and SSM/retrieval where long‑range compression/reuse is best, improving perplexity/accuracy at equal or lower cost.
- SLO robustness across devices: CVaR‑based training with live, device‑calibrated LUTs yields models that consistently meet p95/p99 constraints on diverse GPUs/TPUs under changing length/batch distributions.
- Length‑robust hybridization: Structured SSM/retrieval routing with timescale regularization preserves performance at much longer contexts while constraining unstructured filters.
- Operator control and debuggability: Exposed per‑token/head routing/SKU traces and dual variables enable on‑the‑fly budget retuning without full retraining and make routes auditable.
- Broad applicability: Drop‑in for JAX BST and PyTorch Mamba/DeltaNet stacks; extends to speech/vision encoders and RAG; router subsumes MoE within branches to enable budgeted hybrid‑MoE under the same scheduler.

experimental_design.experiment_strategy:
Title: LAMBS++ validation via three complementary experiments (tail latency + Pareto quality + length generalization)

Overview
- We propose three Python-feasible experiments that isolate (1) runtime routing+scheduler tail benefits, (2) quality–efficiency Pareto gains at fixed p95 budgets on real tasks, and (3) length-generalization and routing interpretability.
- All reuse shared components: RouterR (hierarchical, discrete SKUs with straight-through Gumbel), DiscreteSKUManager, CostProfiler (LUT), KernelScheduler (speculative, bucketed), BudgetController (Lagrangian CVaR), and logging.

Common environment
- Python 3.10, PyTorch 2.3+ CUDA 12.x, Triton 2.1, flash-attn 2.5+, mamba-ssm (or causal-conv1d fallback), FAISS-gpu (optional retrieval), pynvml.
- Single A100/RTX 4090; optional TPU path can be added later (not required for these experiments).

Experiment 1 — Tail-aware runtime benefits on heterogeneous-length batches
Goal
- Demonstrate that per-token/head discrete routing plus the speculative scheduler translates into lower p95/p99 latency and energy at the same or better task loss vs. static hybrids, and that CVaR-based training tightens tails.

Setup
- Model: Small 12-layer hybrid block (hidden=768, n_heads=12). Each block has a parallel attention branch and an SSM branch; outputs fused by per-head gated-add.
- Branch SKUs (discrete kernels):
  • Attention window W in {256, 512, 1024} via FlashAttention with causal local masks.
  • SSM state pack S in {8, 16, 32} via mamba-ssm selective_scan or a CUDA Triton kernel; each SKU keeps a preconfigured multi-timescale parameterization.
  • Retrieval k in {0, 2} with a tiny FAISS flat index on a small in-memory corpus; integrate only to stress routing (can be disabled for this experiment).
- Router: 2-stage hierarchical — Stage A per head-group chooses SKU subset; Stage B per token/head chooses {branch, SKU} and gates. Discretization via straight-through Gumbel; fragmentation loss to encourage contiguous segments.
- Scheduler: Bucket by (branch, SKU, head-group), capture CUDA Graphs per bucket shape; R_{t+1} predict-ahead used to pre-batch; fallback path for mispred.
- Workload: Synthetic language modeling on a wordpiece toy corpus (or WikiText-2) with strongly heterogeneous lengths. Batch sampler mixes seq_len ∈ {128, 256, 512, 1024, 2048} with a Zipfian skew. Train for ~50k steps to stabilize routing and LUT.

Baselines
- Static hybrid: fixed W=1024 attention + SSM S=32 always active; no router, no scheduler.
- Soft router without discrete SKUs: continuous gates but no per-token SKU choice; run superset kernels each step.
- LAMBS++ w/o scheduler: do routing but launch all kernels (mask-inefficient) to isolate scheduler impact.
- LAMBS++ mean-latency objective: remove CVaR and Lagrangian.

Metrics
- p50/p95/p99 latency per sequence/token (CUDA events averaged over N steps; CVaR_0.95 estimate via top-5% mean).
- Energy/token (pynvml power sampling integrated over step). Memory BW proxy (pynvml mem util + modeled bytes/sku).
- Task quality: LM validation perplexity.
- Router stats: distribution over SKUs, fragmentation (avg run-length), misprediction/fallback rate, scheduler overhead.

Protocol
1) Warmup LUT: run micro-bench per SKU and shape to fill CostProfiler; record median and p95 latency per kernel.
2) Phase 1 (explore): train with entropy bonus, λ≈0, teacher prior from full attention+SSM; 5k steps.
3) Phase 2 (tighten): enable discrete SKUs, fragmentation penalty, CVaR-based Lagrangian to target SLO p95 (e.g., 3.0 ms/token on 4090); 40k steps.
4) Evaluate on held-out heterogeneous batches; repeat runs ×3 for variance.

Success criteria
- ≥20–30% reduction in p95/p99 latency vs static hybrid at comparable perplexity.
- Lower energy/token (≥10–20%).
- With CVaR: narrower tail (p99/p50 ratio reduced) compared to mean-latency objective.
- LAMBS++ w/o scheduler underperforms full LAMBS++, isolating scheduler contribution.

Experiment 2 — Quality–efficiency Pareto on real long-context tasks at matched p95 budgets
Goal
- Show that for equal p95 latency/energy budgets, LAMBS++ achieves better perplexity/accuracy by allocating attention locally and SSM/retrieval for long-range, compared to attention-only, SSM-only, and static hybrids.

Setup
- Tasks: PG19 language modeling (perplexity) and LRA Retrieval or LongBench subsets (QA/F1 or accuracy). Context lengths up to 8k–16k.
- Models: 350M-700M parameter class hybrid (8–16 layers). Training or fine-tuning for 100k–200k steps on each dataset.
- Routing/Kernels: same SKU sets as Exp1 plus optional retrieval k∈{0,2,4} for LongBench QA.
- Device SLOs: define two budgets per device (e.g., A100 and 4090): p95 ms/token targets and energy/token caps derived from LUT medians.

Baselines
- Attention-only (FlashAttention local/global W fixed) matched to budget via window size.
- SSM-only (Mamba/DeltaNet) tuned state size to hit budget.
- Static hybrid (fixed W and S across tokens/heads).
- LAMBS++ ablations: no retrieval; no hierarchical router; no SKU choice.

Metrics
- Quality: PPL on PG19; accuracy/F1 on LongBench/LRA.
- Efficiency: p50/p95 latency, energy/token; memory BW proxy.
- Stability: misprediction rate; LUT drift over training; budget violation rate.

Protocol
1) Calibrate LUT on target device(s).
2) For each method, tune hyperparameters to meet the same p95 target (Lagrangian for LAMBS++ vs manual tuning baselines); verify constraints over 10k eval sequences with mixed lengths.
3) Plot Quality vs p95 curves (Pareto). Also report accuracy by distance bin (near vs far tokens) to show selective attention.

Success criteria
- LAMBS++ dominates or matches Pareto frontier: improves quality by ≥0.5–1.5 PPL (PG19) or ≥2–5 points on QA/F1 at matched p95.
- Lower budget violation rate and better cross-device transfer (recalibrated LUT) than static methods.

Experiment 3 — Length generalization, routing interpretability, and safeguards
Goal
- Validate that LAMBS++ routes far-range to SSM/RAG, caps unstructured filters, and maintains stability at much longer contexts via SKU packs and spectral regularization.

Setup
- Synthetic: Needle-in-a-Haystack and Copy/Associative Recall with lengths up to 64k.
- Real: RULER or LongBench Needle/PassageRetrieval subsets up to 32k–64k.
- Curriculum: 4k → 16k → 64k context during fine-tuning; enable eigenvalue clipping and multi-timescale SKU packs.

Baselines
- Static hybrid; attention-only with larger windows; SSM-only scaled.

Metrics
- Accuracy vs distance bins; failure rate beyond training lengths; stability (no NaNs, bounded spectral radius); routing traces by distance; memory BW.
- Efficiency tails: p95/p99 latency under long contexts.

Protocol
1) Train with length curriculum + safeguards; log routing histograms by relative position.
2) Evaluate accuracy at 2× and 4× training lengths; record whether router shifts mass to SSM/RAG as distance grows.
3) Ablate safeguards: remove spectral clipping or cap on max S choice; observe drift and failures.

Success criteria
- Sustained accuracy at 2×–4× lengths compared to significant drops in baselines.
- Interpretable routing: near tokens → attention SKU; far → SSM/RAG; reduced fragmentation.


Shared implementation sketch (Python)

Environment and deps
- pip install: torch torchvision triton pynvml flash-attn --no-build-isolation
- optional: pip install mamba-ssm causal-conv1d faiss-gpu

Data utilities (heterogeneous length sampler)

class MixedLengthLM:
    def __init__(self, tokenizer, lengths=(128,256,512,1024,2048), probs=(0.3,0.25,0.2,0.15,0.1)):
        self.lengths = lengths; self.probs = probs; self.tok = tokenizer
    def batch(self, B):
        import numpy as np
        L = np.random.choice(self.lengths, p=self.probs)
        # Create a toy autoregressive batch (replace with real corpus iterator)
        x = torch.randint(low=0, high=self.tok.vocab_size, size=(B, L), device='cuda')
        return x[:, :-1], x[:, 1:]


Discrete SKU manager

class DiscreteSKUManager:
    def __init__(self, attn_windows=(256,512,1024), ssm_states=(8,16,32), retr_k=(0,2)):
        self.attn_windows = list(attn_windows)
        self.ssm_states = list(ssm_states)
        self.retr_k = list(retr_k)
        # Enumerate SKU ids per branch
        self.attn_ids = list(range(len(self.attn_windows)))
        self.ssm_ids = list(range(len(self.ssm_states)))
        self.retr_ids = list(range(len(self.retr_k)))


Cost profiler (LUT micro-bench + online updates)

class CostProfiler:
    def __init__(self, sku_mgr: DiscreteSKUManager):
        import pynvml
        pynvml.nvmlInit()
        self.sku = sku_mgr
        self.lut = {}  # key: (branch, sku_id, shape, dtype) -> dict(latency_ms_p50, p95, energy_mJ)
    @torch.no_grad()
    def profile_attention(self, L, B, H, D, W, repeats=30):
        # Build a local attention op using FlashAttention v2 with window W
        from flash_attn import flash_attn_func
        q = torch.randn(B, L, H, D, device='cuda', dtype=torch.float16)
        k = torch.randn_like(q)
        v = torch.randn_like(q)
        mask = None  # pass local window via custom kernel args or mask indices
        start = torch.cuda.Event(enable_timing=True)
        end = torch.cuda.Event(enable_timing=True)
        times = []
        for _ in range(repeats):
            torch.cuda.synchronize()
            start.record()
            _ = flash_attn_func(q, k, v, causal=True)  # adapt for window by masking indices
            end.record(); end.synchronize()
            times.append(start.elapsed_time(end))
        lat = torch.tensor(times)
        return lat.median().item(), lat.kthvalue(int(0.95*repeats)).values.item()
    def profile_all(self, shapes):
        # shapes: list of (L, B, H, D)
        for (L,B,H,D) in shapes:
            for i,W in enumerate(self.sku.attn_windows):
                p50,p95 = self.profile_attention(L,B,H,D,W)
                self.lut[("attn", i, (L,B,H,D), torch.float16)] = {"p50_ms":p50, "p95_ms":p95}
            for j,S in enumerate(self.sku.ssm_states):
                # TODO: implement ssm microbench; placeholder proxy numbers or implement mamba-ssm selective_scan
                self.lut[("ssm", j, (L,B,H,D), torch.float16)] = {"p50_ms": max(0.05, 0.2*L/1024), "p95_ms": max(0.1, 0.25*L/1024)}
            for k,kdepth in enumerate(self.sku.retr_k):
                self.lut[("retr", k, (L,B,H,D), torch.float16)] = {"p50_ms": 0.02*kdepth, "p95_ms": 0.03*kdepth}
    def query(self, branch, sku_id, shape, dtype=torch.float16, tail=False):
        rec = self.lut.get((branch, sku_id, shape, dtype))
        if rec is None:
            # fallback interpolation or conservative estimate
            return {"ms": 1.0}
        return {"ms": rec["p95_ms"] if tail else rec["p50_ms"]}


RouterR (hierarchical router with straight-through Gumbel and fragmentation regularizer)

class RouterR(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model, n_heads, head_groups=3, sku_mgr=None):
        super().__init__()
        self.d_model = d_model; self.n_heads = n_heads; self.G = head_groups
        self.sku = sku_mgr or DiscreteSKUManager()
        self.group_map = torch.tensor([i % self.G for i in range(n_heads)], device='cuda')
        self.stageA = torch.nn.Linear(d_model, self.G * (2 + 3))  # logits to choose branch subset per group (2 branches + up to 3 SKU ids)
        self.stageB = torch.nn.Linear(d_model, n_heads * (2 + max(len(self.sku.attn_ids), len(self.sku.ssm_ids))))
        self.hysteresis = torch.nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(n_heads))
    def forward(self, x, cheap_taps=None, temperature=1.0):
        # x: [B, L, D]
        B,L,D = x.shape
        feat = x  # could concat cheap taps
        a_logits = self.stageA(feat.mean(dim=1))  # [B, G*(...)] coarse
        # Stage A: choose small subsets per group (omitted: mapping to actual sku subsets)
        b_logits = self.stageB(x)  # [B, L, n_heads*(...)] fine per token/head
        # Build categorical branch choice per token/head with straigh-through Gumbel
        def st_gumbel_softmax(logits, tau):
            u = torch.rand_like(logits)
            g = -torch.log(-torch.log(u+1e-9)+1e-9)
            y = torch.softmax((logits+g)/tau, dim=-1)
            # Straight-through: hard max in forward
            y_hard = torch.zeros_like(y)
            y_hard.scatter_(-1, y.max(dim=-1, keepdim=True).indices, 1.0)
            return (y_hard - y).detach() + y
        # For brevity, we output: branch_idx in {0:attn,1:ssm}, sku_idx per branch, and mixing gates per head
        branch_logits = b_logits[..., :self.n_heads*2].view(B,L,self.n_heads,2)
        branch_sel = st_gumbel_softmax(branch_logits, temperature)
        sku_logits = b_logits[..., self.n_heads*2:]
        # Map sku_logits into attn/ssm sku id distributions per head (simplified)
        attn_sku_sel = st_gumbel_softmax(sku_logits[..., :self.n_heads*len(self.sku.attn_ids)].view(B,L,self.n_heads,len(self.sku.attn_ids)), temperature)
        ssm_sku_sel = st_gumbel_softmax(sku_logits[..., -self.n_heads*len(self.sku.ssm_ids):].view(B,L,self.n_heads,len(self.sku.ssm_ids)), temperature)
        # Gates per head
        gate_logits = torch.nn.functional.linear(x, torch.empty(self.n_heads, D, device=x.device).normal_(0,0.02))
        gates = torch.sigmoid(gate_logits).unsqueeze(-1).repeat(1,1,1,2)  # dummy 2-branch gate
        return branch_sel, attn_sku_sel, ssm_sku_sel, gates


Hybrid block (parallel attention + SSM branches; outputs gated-add by router)

class HybridBlock(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_model=768, n_heads=12, sku_mgr=None):
        super().__init__()
        self.d_model = d_model; self.n_heads = n_heads
        self.sku = sku_mgr or DiscreteSKUManager()
        from flash_attn.modules.mha import MHA
        self.attn = MHA(embed_dim=d_model, num_heads=n_heads, dropout=0.0, causal=True)
        try:
            from mamba_ssm import Mamba  # or a proxy SSM
            self.ssm = Mamba(d_model)
        except Exception:
            self.ssm = torch.nn.Conv1d(d_model, d_model, kernel_size=9, groups=d_model, padding=8, dilation=2)  # proxy
        self.router = RouterR(d_model, n_heads, sku_mgr=self.sku)
        self.out = torch.nn.Linear(d_model, d_model)
    def forward(self, x):  # x: [B,L,D]
        B,L,D = x.shape
        branch_sel, attn_sku_sel, ssm_sku_sel, gates = self.router(x)
        # Scheduler will decide which kernels to run; here we compute both for clarity (for ablation w/o scheduler)
        y_attn = self.attn(x)
        y_ssm = self.ssm(x.transpose(1,2)).transpose(1,2) if not hasattr(self.ssm, 'forward_states') else self.ssm(x)
        g_attn = gates[...,0].mean(dim=2)  # [B,L]
        g_ssm = gates[...,1].mean(dim=2)
        y = g_attn.unsqueeze(-1)*y_attn + g_ssm.unsqueeze(-1)*y_ssm
        return self.out(y), {"branch_sel": branch_sel, "attn_sku_sel": attn_sku_sel, "ssm_sku_sel": ssm_sku_sel}


KernelScheduler (predict-then-execute bucketing)

class KernelScheduler:
    def __init__(self, sku_mgr: DiscreteSKUManager):
        self.sku = sku_mgr
        self.stats = {"fallbacks": 0, "overhead_ms": 0.0}
    def run(self, x, router_outputs, attn_op, ssm_op):
        # x: [B,L,D]; router_outputs contain hard one-hots for branch/skus per token/head
        # For simplicity, approximate bucket by majority SKU per head across tokens (reduce fragmentation)
        branch = router_outputs["branch_sel"]  # [B,L,H,2]
        attn_sku = router_outputs["attn_sku_sel"]  # [B,L,H,|A|]
        ssm_sku = router_outputs["ssm_sku_sel"]    # [B,L,H,|S|]
        # Derive per-head SKU ids by modal selection
        attn_ids = attn_sku.argmax(-1).mode(dim=1).values  # [B,H]
        ssm_ids = ssm_sku.argmax(-1).mode(dim=1).values
        branch_ids = branch.argmax(-1).mode(dim=1).values  # [B,H]
        # Bucket heads
        buckets = {}
        H = branch_ids.shape[-1]
        for h in range(H):
            b = int(branch_ids[0,h].item())
            if b==0:
                key=("attn", int(attn_ids[0,h].item()))
            else:
                key=("ssm", int(ssm_ids[0,h].item()))
            buckets.setdefault(key, []).append(h)
        outputs = []
        for (branch_name, sku_id), heads in buckets.items():
            # Slice heads and run the corresponding kernel (here we call generic ops; real impl would pass SKU to kernel)
            if branch_name=="attn":
                y = attn_op(x, heads=heads, window=self.sku.attn_windows[sku_id])
            else:
                y = ssm_op(x, heads=heads, state=self.sku.ssm_states[sku_id])
            outputs.append((heads, y))
        # Merge by head order; placeholder: average
        y_merge = sum(y for _,y in outputs) / max(1,len(outputs))
        return y_merge


Budget controller with CVaR tail objective

class BudgetController:
    def __init__(self, target_ms_token=3.0, alpha=0.95, lr_lambda=1e-2):
        self.target = target_ms_token; self.alpha = alpha
        self.lmbda = torch.nn.Parameter(torch.tensor(0.0), requires_grad=False)
        self.lr_lambda = lr_lambda
    def cvar(self, latencies_ms):
        # latencies_ms: [batch] per-sequence or per-token
        k = max(1, int((1.0-self.alpha)*latencies_ms.numel()))
        topk, _ = torch.topk(latencies_ms, k)
        return topk.mean()
    def penalty(self, latencies_ms):
        c = self.cvar(latencies_ms)
        return self.lmbda * torch.relu(c - self.target)
    def update_lambda(self, observed_ms):
        err = observed_ms - self.target
